pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.05Mlkg: 0.5 mL/kg/hr over last 4 hours (borderline — normal minimum for children: 1 mL/kg/hr).
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.20Mlkg: 20 mL/kg NS bolus (14 kg × 20 = 280 mL) over 5–10 minutes. Reassess after each bolus — may repeat up to 60 mL/kg in the first hour. Assess for fluid responsiveness: improved heart rate, perfusion, mental status, urine output.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.30Mlkg: 30 mL/kg crystalloid (typically NS or LR) within 3 hours. For this 65 kg patient: ~2000 mL bolus. Reassess fluid responsiveness after each bolus.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.5IfMap: 5. If MAP remains < 65 mmHg after fluids:
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.activa: Activate rapid response or pediatric emergency team. This child shows signs of compensated septic shock — do not wait for further deterioration.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.acuteE: Acute exacerbation of COPD (AECOPD) with acute-on-chronic type II respiratory failure. Yellow-green sputum and mild leukocytosis suggest an infectious trigger (likely bacterial bronchitis or pneumonia).
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.acuteI: Acute ischemic stroke — likely cardioembolic origin (atrial fibrillation with subtherapeutic anticoagulation). Left middle cerebral artery (MCA) territory based on right hemiparesis, facial droop, and aphasia pattern.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.acuteK: Acute kidney injury (creatinine 1.6 from baseline 0.9), altered mental status, hypotension, elevated lactate — multiple organ systems are showing dysfunction consistent with sepsis.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.acuteP: Acute pericarditis (diffuse ST elevation, no reciprocal changes), aortic dissection (tearing pain, unequal pulses), pulmonary embolism (pleuritic pain, tachycardia, hypoxia).
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.admitt: Admitted 18 hours ago with community-acquired pneumonia. Night nurse reports the child
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.alertB: Alert but confused, follows simple commands inconsistently. Right hemiparesis (arm greater than leg). Right-sided facial droop (lower face). Receptive language partially intact but expressive aphasia present. Right visual field neglect suspected.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.alread: (already obtained: 3.8 mmol/L). Repeat lactate in 2–4 hours. Target: lactate clearance or decrease by ≥ 10%.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.anxiou: Anxious, diaphoretic, clutching chest. Skin pale and clammy.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.atrial: Atrial fibrillation (on warfarin, inconsistent compliance), hypertension, hyperlipidemia, type 2 diabetes.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.bilate: Bilateral breath sounds clear, no crackles. Mild tachypnea.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.bloodC: Blood cultures × 2, CBC with differential, CRP, lactate, BMP (electrolytes, glucose, renal function), blood gas. Obtain before changing antibiotics if possible.
Blood cultures × 2 (from different sites), urine culture, wound culture from surgical site. Do not delay antibiotics more than 45 minutes for cultures.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.bornAt: Born at 35 weeks gestation, no significant neonatal complications. Up to date on immunizations. No chronic conditions. No allergies.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.bp1488: BP 148/88 mmHg, HR 110 bpm, RR 32/min, SpO₂ 86% on room air, Temp 37.8°C
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.bp1589: BP 158/94 mmHg, HR 102 bpm (regular), RR 22/min, SpO₂ 96% on room air, Temp 37.1°C
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.bp1861: BP 186/102 mmHg, HR 88 bpm (irregularly irregular), RR 18/min, SpO₂ 97%, Temp 36.8°C, Blood glucose 142 mg/dL
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.bp8852: BP 88/52 mmHg (baseline 130/78), HR 118 bpm, RR 24/min, SpO₂ 93% on room air, Temp 39.2°C (102.6°F)
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.cardia: Cardiac monitoring, continuous pulse oximetry, strict I&O (weigh diapers), frequent vital signs q15min during acute resuscitation, blood glucose monitoring (children are prone to hypoglycemia during sepsis).
Cardiac telemetry, serial ECGs, repeat troponin at 3 and 6 hours, strict I&O.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.cefazo: Cefazolin 2g IV q8h (surgical prophylaxis), enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily, oxycodone 5 mg PO q4h PRN, metformin (held post-op), lisinopril 10 mg daily.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.childr: Children compensate for shock much longer than adults, maintaining blood pressure until very late. By the time hypotension occurs in a child, they are in decompensated shock — a pre-arrest state. The key is recognizing early signs: tachycardia, altered mental status, poor perfusion, and decreased urine output.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.confus: Confused, restless, appears ill. Skin warm and flushed with poor turgor.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.contin: Continuous nebulized albuterol 2.5 mg + ipratropium 0.5 mg q20min × 3, then q4h. Consider adding IV magnesium sulfate if severe bronchospasm.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.copdDi: COPD (diagnosed 8 years ago), 40 pack-year smoking history (current smoker), hypertension, anxiety. Two prior COPD exacerbations requiring hospitalization in the past year.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.ctComp: CT completed within 10 minutes of arrival (achieved). Rule out hemorrhagic stroke before considering thrombolytics.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.decrea: Decreased cardiac output related to myocardial ischemia/infarction as evidenced by ST elevation, elevated troponin, chest pain, tachycardia, and diaphoresis.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.dimini: Diminished breath sounds bilaterally with scattered expiratory wheezes and rhonchi. Prolonged expiratory phase. Barrel chest configuration. No stridor.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.doorto: — door-to-balloon time goal < 90 minutes for primary PCI.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.dose09: Dose: 0.9 mg/kg (max 90 mg). Give 10% as IV bolus over 1 minute, then infuse remaining 90% over 60 minutes. Start within 60 minutes of arrival.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.explai: Explain procedures, provide emotional support, keep family informed.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.faceLe: Face — left-sided facial droop when smiling. Arms — right arm drifts downward and cannot maintain against gravity. Speech — dysarthric, word-finding difficulty, unable to repeat sentences accurately. Time — symptom onset 30 minutes ago.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.foleyC: Foley catheter in place — urine cloudy and concentrated, output 15 mL in last 2 hours.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.forTpa: For tPA candidates, BP must be < 185/110 before and < 180/105 during/after tPA. Current BP 186/102 — administer IV labetalol 10–20 mg to lower BP below threshold.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.hepari: Heparin bolus + drip per protocol, clopidogrel/ticagrelor loading dose, beta-blocker (metoprolol) if HR > 100 and no contraindications.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.hr168B: HR 168 bpm (normal for age: 80–120), RR 42/min (normal: 20–30), SpO₂ 90% on 2L nasal cannula (was 96% on admission), Temp 39.8°C (103.6°F), BP 78/45 mmHg (normal minimum SBP: 70 + [2 × age] = 76 mmHg — borderline low)
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.hypert: Hypertension (10 years), hyperlipidemia, type 2 diabetes, former smoker (quit 5 years ago), family history of MI (father at age 58).
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.iHaveA: "I have a crushing pain in my chest that won
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.increa: Increase to high-flow nasal cannula or non-rebreather mask to target SpO₂ > 94%. Position upright. Be prepared for respiratory support (CPAP, intubation) if grunting worsens or mental status declines further.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.initia: Initiate empiric antibiotics (e.g., azithromycin or levofloxacin) given purulent sputum and suspected bacterial trigger.
Initiate vasopressor therapy (norepinephrine first-line). May require ICU transfer.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.inr16S: INR 1.6 (subtherapeutic — target 2.0–3.0 on warfarin), glucose 142 mg/dL, CBC within normal limits.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.keepNp: Keep NPO until bedside swallow evaluation completed. Dysphagia screening is mandatory before any oral intake (aspiration risk).
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.keepPa: Keep parents informed and at bedside when possible. Explain interventions and what to expect. Parents know their child best — their concern that the child "doesn
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.lastKn: Last known well time 30 minutes ago — well within the 4.5-hour window for IV alteplase. CT shows no hemorrhage. INR 1.6 (alteplase can be given if INR < 1.7). No recent surgery, bleeding, or other absolute contraindications identified.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.lisino: Lisinopril 20 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg daily, metformin 1000 mg BID.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.listle: Listless, not making eye contact, not interested in toys. Responds to voice but irritable when touched. Not crying with tears — mucous membranes tacky. Skin mottled on extremities, capillary refill 4 seconds.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.methyl: Methylprednisolone 125 mg IV or prednisone 40 mg PO. Reduces airway inflammation and shortens recovery time.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.miaWas: Mia was admitted with 3-day history of cough, fever, and decreased oral intake. Initial chest X-ray showed right lower lobe consolidation. Started on IV ampicillin. Was playful and interactive on admission but has become progressively less active over the past 6 hours. Parents report she has not eaten and has had minimal fluid intake.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.morphi: Morphine 2–4 mg IV PRN (if pain not relieved by NTG), Oxygen only if SpO₂ < 94%, Nitroglycerin 0.4 mg SL q5min × 3 (hold if SBP < 90), Aspirin 325 mg chewed (already taken by patient).
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.neuroC: Neuro checks q15min × 2h, then q30min × 6h, then q1h × 16h. Strict BP monitoring — keep < 180/105. Monitor for signs of hemorrhagic conversion (new headache, vomiting, acute neuro decline).
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.noHemo: No hemorrhage identified. No early ischemic changes.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.normal: Normal BNP rules out heart failure as a contributing factor. The clinical picture is consistent with infectious COPD exacerbation.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.notify: Notify neurologist/stroke team immediately. Prepare for IV alteplase (tPA) administration.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.nursin: Nursing Clinical Scenarios — Interactive Case Studies
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.obtain: Obtain before starting antibiotics if possible.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.osteoa: Osteoarthritis, hypertension, type 2 diabetes (A1C 7.8%), urinary tract infections (recurrent).
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.painSt: Pain started 45 minutes ago while watching television. Substernal, radiating to the left arm and jaw. Rates pain 8/10. Describes it as "heavy" and "squeezing." Associated with diaphoresis and nausea. Took one aspirin at home before calling 911.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.patien: Patient was at baseline 30 minutes ago. Wife reports he suddenly dropped his fork, his right arm went limp, and he began speaking incoherently. He is able to follow simple commands inconsistently. Last known well time: 12:15 PM. Current time: 12:45 PM.

Patient was progressing normally on POD 1 and 2. On POD 3 morning, she became increasingly confused and oriented only to person. Temperature spiked to 39.2°C. She reports feeling "cold" despite the fever. Surgical wound has increased redness and warmth around incision edges.

Patient with known COPD (GOLD Stage III) presents to the ED with worsening dyspnea over the past 3 days. Reports increased sputum production (yellow-green) and a productive cough. Used his rescue inhaler 8 times today with minimal relief. Cannot speak in full sentences. Was sitting in tripod position upon arrival.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.ph731A: pH 7.31 = acidosis. PaCO₂ 58 = elevated (respiratory acidosis). HCO₃ 32 = elevated (chronic compensation). PaO₂ 54 = severe hypoxemia. This is a partially compensated respiratory acidosis with acute-on-chronic respiratory failure. The elevated HCO₃ indicates pre-existing chronic CO₂ retention (baseline COPD) with acute worsening.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.ph731P: pH 7.31, PaCO₂ 58 mmHg, HCO₃ 32 mEq/L, PaO₂ 54 mmHg
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.postop: Post-operative day 3 following right total hip arthroplasty. Nurse noted increasing confusion and elevated temperature during routine assessment.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.practi: Practice clinical reasoning with interactive nursing scenarios. Chest pain, sepsis, respiratory distress, stroke, and pediatric deterioration cases with assessment findings, nursing priorities, and quiz questions.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.repeat: Repeat ABG in 1–2 hours. If PaCO₂ continues to rise, pH drops below 7.25, or mental status worsens → prepare for non-invasive ventilation (BiPAP) or intubation. BiPAP is first-line for COPD exacerbation with respiratory acidosis.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.rightH: Right hip incision with erythema extending 3 cm beyond staple line, warmth, and small amount of purulent drainage.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.s1s2He: S1/S2 heard, no murmurs. Regular tachycardia. No JVD.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.sittin: Sitting upright, tripod position, using accessory muscles (sternocleidomastoid, intercostals), pursed-lip breathing, speaks 2–3 word sentences only. Appears fatigued.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.stElev: ST elevation in leads II, III, aVF (2–3 mm). Reciprocal ST depression in leads I and aVL.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.startO: Start O₂ via Venturi mask at 28% — titrate to target SpO₂ 88–92%. COPD patients rely on hypoxic drive; excessive O₂ can suppress respiratory drive and worsen CO₂ retention.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.strict: Strict I&O (urine output goal ≥ 0.5 mL/kg/hr), continuous cardiac monitoring, frequent vital signs (q15 min during resuscitation), neuro checks q1h.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.subcos: Subcostal and intercostal retractions, nasal flaring, grunting on expiration. Diminished breath sounds right lower lobe with crackles. Increased work of breathing compared to admission.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.surgic: Surgical site infection (SSI) with purulent drainage and expanding erythema. Concurrent UTI possible given cloudy urine, indwelling catheter, and history of recurrent UTIs. Both sources require cultures before broadening antibiotic coverage.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.tachyc: Tachycardic, weak peripheral pulses. Cool extremities despite fever.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.theCli: The clinical picture suggests progression from localized pneumonia to possible sepsis with impending septic shock. The hemodynamic changes (tachycardia, borderline BP, poor perfusion) suggest compensated shock — the body is maintaining blood pressure through tachycardia, but decompensation is imminent if not treated aggressively.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.theCur: The current regimen (ampicillin alone) may be insufficient. Notify the provider to consider broadening to ceftriaxone or adding vancomycin if MRSA is suspected.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.theStE: The ST elevation in inferior leads (II, III, aVF) with reciprocal changes in lateral leads indicates an inferior STEMI. The elevated troponin confirms myocardial injury. Risk factors (age, male, HTN, DM, hyperlipidemia, smoking history, family history) create a high pretest probability for ACS. The 45-minute symptom duration places this patient within the PCI window.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.thisCh: This child is showing multiple red flags for clinical deterioration: rising heart rate, worsening respiratory distress (retractions, grunting, nasal flaring), declining oxygen saturation despite supplemental O₂, altered behavior (listless, not interactive), poor perfusion (mottled skin, prolonged cap refill, cool extremities, weak pulses), decreased urine output, and persistent high fever.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.thisPa: This patient meets SIRS criteria (temp > 38.3°C, HR > 90, RR > 20, WBC > 12,000) with a suspected infection source (surgical site and/or UTI). The qSOFA score of 2 (altered mentation + hypotension) indicates high risk for poor outcome. Elevated lactate (3.8 mmol/L) indicates tissue hypoperfusion — this patient has sepsis with organ dysfunction progressing toward septic shock.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.timeIs: "Time is brain" — approximately 1.9 million neurons are lost per minute during an acute ischemic stroke. Every minute of delay in reperfusion worsens outcomes. Door-to-needle time goal: < 60 minutes.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.tiotro: Tiotropium (Spiriva) 18 mcg daily, fluticasone/salmeterol (Advair) 250/50 BID, albuterol (Ventolin) PRN, lisinopril 10 mg daily.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.toOpti: to optimize cerebral perfusion and reduce ICP risk.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.tropon: Troponin I: 0.8 ng/mL (elevated), BNP: 180 pg/mL, glucose: 185 mg/dL.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.twoLar: Two large-bore IVs (18G or larger). Draw labs: troponin, CK-MB, CBC, BMP, coagulation studies, type & screen.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.warfar: Warfarin 5 mg daily, metoprolol 50 mg BID, atorvastatin 80 mg daily, metformin 500 mg BID.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.wbc182: WBC 18,200/μL, lactate 3.8 mmol/L, creatinine 1.6 mg/dL (baseline 0.9), glucose 245 mg/dL, procalcitonin 4.2 ng/mL.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.wifeCa: Wife called 911 reporting that her husband suddenly developed right-sided weakness and "his speech doesn
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.within: within 1 hour of sepsis recognition. Notify surgeon and infectious disease. Broaden from cefazolin to cover both SSI and UTI sources (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam + vancomycin).

pages.nursingStudyGuides.addressUnderlyi: Address underlying cause, coach slow breathing, provide reassurance for anxiety-related hyperventilation, adjust ventilator settings if mechanically ventilated.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.alwaysDocumentT: Always document the time the critical value was received, the time the provider was notified, the provider
pages.nursingStudyGuides.anxietypanicAtt: Anxiety/panic attacks, pain, fever, early salicylate poisoning, mechanical overventilation, hypoxia-driven tachypnea.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.areTheMostAccur: are the most accurate indicator of fluid status changes. A gain of 1 kg (2.2 lbs) = approximately 1 liter of fluid retention.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.assessForSignsO: Assess for signs of stroke (FAST: Face drooping, Arm weakness, Speech difficulty, Time to call 911). A-fib is the most common cause of cardioembolic stroke.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.below735Acidosi: Below 7.35 = acidosis. Above 7.45 = alkalosis. Normal pH with abnormal CO₂ and HCO₃ = fully compensated.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.calciumAndPhosp: Calcium and phosphorus have an inverse relationship — when one goes up, the other goes down. Always check both values together.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.causeWaterToShi: Cause water to shift from ECF into cells (ICF). Examples: 0.45% Half-Normal Saline (½NS), 0.225% Quarter-Normal Saline. Use for: cellular dehydration, hypernatremia. Caution: never give to patients with increased ICP (can worsen cerebral edema).
pages.nursingStudyGuides.causesIncludeAl: Causes include alcoholism, malnutrition, diuretics, diarrhea. Signs: tremors, hyperreflexia, tetany, seizures, cardiac arrhythmias (torsades de pointes). Nursing: IV magnesium sulfate (with calcium gluconate at bedside as antidote), monitor deep tendon reflexes, respiratory rate, and urine output.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.causesIncludeDe: Causes include dehydration, diabetes insipidus, excessive sodium intake. Signs: thirst, dry mucous membranes, restlessness, seizures. Nursing: gradual rehydration with hypotonic fluids, monitor for cerebral edema, strict I&O monitoring.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.causesIncludeDi: Causes include diuretics (furosemide), vomiting, diarrhea, alkalosis. Signs: muscle weakness, leg cramps, diminished reflexes, cardiac arrhythmias (flattened T waves, U waves, ST depression). Nursing: oral or IV potassium replacement (never push IV potassium), monitor ECG, assess digoxin toxicity risk.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.causesIncludeHy: Causes include hyperparathyroidism, malignancy, immobility, excessive vitamin D. Signs: "bones, stones, moans, groans" (bone pain, kidney stones, confusion, GI symptoms). Nursing: hydration with NS, loop diuretics, calcitonin, bisphosphonates.
Causes include hypoparathyroidism, vitamin D deficiency, pancreatitis, renal failure. Signs: Trousseau
pages.nursingStudyGuides.causesIncludeRe: Causes include refeeding syndrome, antacid use, alcoholism, DKA treatment. Signs: muscle weakness, respiratory failure, confusion, rhabdomyolysis. Nursing: oral or IV phosphorus replacement, monitor respiratory status.

Causes include renal failure, ACE inhibitors, potassium-sparing diuretics, burns, crush injuries. Signs: muscle weakness, paresthesias, cardiac arrhythmias (peaked T waves, widened QRS, sine wave pattern). Nursing: cardiac monitoring, administer calcium gluconate (cardioprotective), insulin + dextrose, kayexalate, prepare for dialysis if severe.

Causes include renal failure, excessive magnesium intake (antacids, laxatives). Signs: decreased DTRs, hypotension, respiratory depression, cardiac arrest. Nursing: stop magnesium, administer calcium gluconate, prepare for dialysis.

Causes include renal failure, tumor lysis syndrome, hypoparathyroidism. Signs: often asymptomatic; may cause symptoms of hypocalcemia (calcium-phosphorus inverse relationship). Nursing: phosphate binders with meals, dietary phosphorus restriction.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.causesIncludeSi: Causes include SIADH, heart failure, excessive hypotonic IV fluids, and diuretic use. Signs: confusion, headache, nausea, seizures (severe). Nursing: fluid restriction, monitor I&O, assess neuro status, administer hypertonic saline cautiously if severe (risk of osmotic demyelination if corrected too rapidly).
pages.nursingStudyGuides.chaoticDisorgan: Chaotic, disorganized electrical activity — no effective cardiac output. Immediately defibrillate. CPR until defibrillator ready. Epinephrine + amiodarone per ACLS protocol.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.completeAvDisso: Complete AV dissociation — P waves and QRS complexes are independent. Wide QRS with slow ventricular rate. Emergency: transcutaneous pacing, atropine, prepare for permanent pacemaker.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.comprehensiveNu: Comprehensive nursing study guides covering electrolytes, acid-base disorders, ECG interpretation, fluid balance, and critical lab values. Aligned with NCLEX and REX-PN exam blueprints.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.confusionMuscle: Confusion, muscle twitching, hand tremors, hypoventilation (compensatory — shallow breathing).
pages.nursingStudyGuides.consistentEcgAn: Consistent ECG analysis requires a systematic approach. Use the same sequence every time to avoid missing critical findings. The recommended approach: Rate → Rhythm → P waves → PR interval → QRS complex → ST segment → T waves.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.consistentPrInt: Consistent PR intervals with sudden dropped QRS complexes. More serious — can progress to complete heart block. May require pacing.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.copdRespiratory: COPD, respiratory depression (opioids, sedation), pneumonia, airway obstruction, neuromuscular diseases (Guillain-Barré, myasthenia gravis).
pages.nursingStudyGuides.criticalOrPanic: Critical (or "panic") lab values are results that fall outside the normal range to a degree that poses an immediate threat to patient safety. When a critical value is identified, the laboratory must notify the nurse or provider immediately, and the nurse must take appropriate action. Failure to act on critical lab values is a common cause of adverse patient outcomes.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.dkaLacticAcidos: DKA, lactic acidosis, renal failure, diarrhea (loss of bicarbonate), toxic ingestions (methanol, ethylene glycol).
pages.nursingStudyGuides.dyspneaConfusio: Dyspnea, confusion, headache, drowsiness progressing to lethargy, tachycardia.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.electrolyteImba: Electrolyte imbalances are among the most commonly tested topics on nursing licensing exams. Understanding normal ranges, clinical manifestations, and appropriate nursing interventions is essential for both exam success and safe clinical practice. Electrolytes are minerals that carry an electrical charge and are essential for nerve conduction, muscle contraction, fluid balance, and acid-base regulation.
Electrolyte imbalances rarely occur in isolation. Hypokalemia often accompanies hypomagnesemia, and correcting the underlying cause is as important as treating the lab value.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.expandTheEcfWit: Expand the ECF without causing fluid shifts between compartments. Examples: 0.9% Normal Saline (NS), Lactated Ringer
pages.nursingStudyGuides.fluidAndSodiumR: Fluid and sodium restriction, administer diuretics (furosemide — monitor potassium), elevate HOB, daily weights, I&O monitoring, oxygen therapy if dyspneic, assess lung sounds frequently.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.heartFailureRen: Heart failure, renal failure, liver cirrhosis, excessive IV fluid administration, SIADH, excessive sodium intake.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.hemorrhageVomit: Hemorrhage, vomiting, diarrhea, excessive diuresis, burns, third-spacing, inadequate fluid intake.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.iWouldLikeToReq: "I would like to request [specific orders: calcium gluconate, insulin/D50, kayexalate, repeat lab in X hours]."
pages.nursingStudyGuides.ifHcoIsAbnormal: If HCO₃ is abnormal and explains the pH change, the primary disorder is metabolic. HCO₃ is a base — decreased HCO₃ causes acidosis.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.ifPacoIsAbnorma: If PaCO₂ is abnormal and explains the pH change, the primary disorder is respiratory. CO₂ is an acid — elevated CO₂ causes acidosis.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.ifTheOtherSyste: If the other system (respiratory or metabolic) is also abnormal and moving in the direction to normalize pH, compensation is occurring.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.improveVentilat: Improve ventilation (positioning, bronchodilators, incentive spirometry), administer supplemental oxygen cautiously in COPD patients, prepare for mechanical ventilation if severe, monitor respiratory rate and depth.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.irregularlyIrre: Irregularly irregular rhythm with no discernible P waves (fibrillatory baseline). Risk of atrial thrombus formation → stroke. Management: rate control (diltiazem, metoprolol), rhythm control (amiodarone, cardioversion), anticoagulation (warfarin, DOACs).
pages.nursingStudyGuides.ivFluidReplacem: IV fluid replacement (isotonic fluids for volume expansion), strict I&O monitoring, daily weights (same time, same scale, same clothing), oral rehydration when tolerated, monitor vital signs for signs of improvement.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.knowThe77RuleFo: Know the "7/7 rule" for transfusion triggers — hemoglobin < 7 g/dL generally triggers RBC transfusion consideration. For platelets, < 10,000 = risk of spontaneous bleeding; < 50,000 = bleeding precautions needed.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.kussmaulRespira: Kussmaul respirations (deep, rapid breathing — compensatory), confusion, fatigue, nausea, abdominal pain.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.lightheadedness: Lightheadedness, numbness/tingling (circumoral and extremities), muscle spasms, palpitations.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.monaMorphineIfN: MONA (Morphine if needed, Oxygen if SpO₂ < 94%, Nitroglycerin, Aspirin 325 mg chewed). Activate cath lab for PCI within 90 minutes of arrival. Obtain serial 12-lead ECGs. Monitor for dysrhythmias.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.normal275295Mos: (normal: 275–295 mOsm/kg) reflects the concentration of solutes in the blood. It is primarily determined by sodium, glucose, and BUN. An elevated osmolality indicates concentrated blood (dehydration), while low osmolality indicates dilute blood (fluid overload or SIADH).
pages.nursingStudyGuides.normalSinusRhyt: Normal sinus rhythm is the baseline against which all other rhythms are compared. Criteria: rate 60–100 bpm, regular rhythm, one P wave before each QRS, PR interval 0.12–0.20 sec, narrow QRS (< 0.12 sec). NSR originates from the SA node and represents normal cardiac conduction.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.nursingStudyGui: Nursing Study Guides — Free Exam Prep Resources
pages.nursingStudyGuides.phGt745WithHco: pH > 7.45 with HCO₃⁻ > 26 mEq/L. The body has excess bicarbonate or lost acid.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.phGt745WithPaco: pH > 7.45 with PaCO₂ < 35 mmHg. The lungs are eliminating too much CO₂ (hyperventilation).
pages.nursingStudyGuides.phLt735WithHco: pH < 7.35 with HCO₃⁻ < 22 mEq/L. The body has excess acid or lost bicarbonate.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.phLt735WithPaco: pH < 7.35 with PaCO₂ > 45 mmHg. The lungs are not eliminating enough CO₂.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.potassiumIsTheM: Potassium is the most commonly tested electrolyte. Remember: IV potassium must be diluted and infused slowly (max 10 mEq/hr peripherally, max 20 mEq/hr centrally). Never push IV potassium — it causes cardiac arrest.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.prIntervalGt020: PR interval > 0.20 sec, every P wave conducts. Usually benign — monitor only.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.progressivePrPr: Progressive PR prolongation until a QRS is dropped. Often transient and asymptomatic. Monitor; usually no treatment needed.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.prolongedVomiti: Prolonged vomiting/NG suction (loss of HCl), excessive antacid use, hypokalemia, diuretic use.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.pullWaterFromCe: Pull water from cells into the ECF. Examples: 3% Saline, D10W, D5NS, D5LR. Use for: severe hyponatremia, cerebral edema (3% saline). Caution: give slowly; risk of fluid volume overload and phlebitis.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.rateGt100BpmWit: Rate > 100 bpm with normal morphology. Causes: fever, pain, anxiety, dehydration, hemorrhage, heart failure. Treatment: address the underlying cause — sinus tachycardia is a symptom, not a primary rhythm disorder.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.rateLt60BpmWith: Rate < 60 bpm with normal P-QRS-T morphology. Common in athletes, during sleep, and with beta-blocker use. Treat with atropine if symptomatic (hypotension, altered mental status). Pacing may be needed for unresponsive symptomatic bradycardia.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.rememberHypoSwe: Remember "hypo = swells cells, hyper = shrinks cells." Never give hypotonic fluids to head injury patients (brain cells swell → increased ICP). Use hypertonic saline cautiously — too-rapid correction of hyponatremia causes osmotic demyelination syndrome.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.replaceChloride: Replace chloride and potassium, administer anti-emetics, discontinue offending medications, monitor respiratory status for compensatory hypoventilation.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.sawtoothPattern: "Sawtooth" pattern of flutter waves, typically at 300 bpm with 2:1, 3:1, or 4:1 conduction. Ventricular rate is regular. Treatment similar to A-fib; cardioversion is often effective.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.stelevationMyoc: ST-elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI) requires immediate recognition and intervention. Look for ST-segment elevation ≥ 1mm in two or more contiguous leads. Territory localization: Inferior (II, III, aVF), Anterior (V1–V4), Lateral (I, aVL, V5–V6). Reciprocal ST depression in opposite leads supports the diagnosis.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.tachycardiaHypo: Tachycardia, hypotension, orthostatic hypotension, decreased urine output (< 30 mL/hr), concentrated urine (specific gravity > 1.030), poor skin turgor, dry mucous membranes, elevated BUN-to-creatinine ratio (> 20:1), weight loss (1 L = 1 kg = 2.2 lbs).
pages.nursingStudyGuides.theBodyMaintain: The body maintains blood pH between 7.35 and 7.45 through three buffering systems: the bicarbonate buffer system, the respiratory system (lungs), and the renal system (kidneys). Disruptions to this balance result in four primary acid-base disorders that nurses must recognize and manage.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.theLungsCompens: The lungs compensate quickly (minutes to hours) by adjusting CO₂ elimination. The kidneys compensate slowly (hours to days) by retaining or excreting bicarbonate.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.thePatientWasAd: "The patient was admitted for [diagnosis]. Relevant history includes [medications, conditions]."
pages.nursingStudyGuides.thePotassiumCam: "The potassium came back at 6.8 mEq/L. The patient
pages.nursingStudyGuides.theRomeMnemonic: The "ROME" mnemonic: Respiratory = Opposite (pH and CO₂ move in opposite directions). Metabolic = Equal (pH and HCO₃ move in the same direction).
pages.nursingStudyGuides.threeOrMoreCons: Three or more consecutive PVCs at rate > 100 bpm. Wide QRS complexes. Pulseless V-tach: defibrillate immediately. V-tach with pulse: amiodarone, synchronized cardioversion if unstable.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.toPracticeStepb: to practice step-by-step acid-base analysis with clinical interpretation.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.totalBodyWaterC: Total body water comprises approximately 60% of body weight in adults (less in elderly and obese patients). This water is distributed between two main compartments: intracellular fluid (ICF, about 2/3) and extracellular fluid (ECF, about 1/3). The ECF is further divided into intravascular (plasma) and interstitial (between cells) compartments.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.treatUnderlying: Treat underlying cause (insulin for DKA, fluids for lactic acidosis), administer sodium bicarbonate if pH < 7.1, monitor potassium (shifts with acidosis correction), assess respiratory effort.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.vfibAndPulseles: V-fib and pulseless V-tach are shockable rhythms. Asystole and PEA are non-shockable. Know the ACLS algorithms for each.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.vomitingCausesM: Vomiting causes metabolic alkalosis (loss of stomach acid). Diarrhea causes metabolic acidosis (loss of bicarbonate from intestines). This distinction is commonly tested.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.waterFollowsSod: "Water follows sodium" — remember that sodium problems are often water problems. SIADH = dilutional hyponatremia (too much water). Diabetes insipidus = hypernatremia (too little water).
pages.nursingStudyGuides.waterMovesBetwe: Water moves between compartments by osmosis — from areas of lower solute concentration to higher solute concentration. Understanding this principle is essential for predicting fluid shifts with IV fluid administration.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.weightGainEdema: Weight gain, edema (peripheral, periorbital, sacral in bedridden patients), distended neck veins (JVD), crackles/rales on auscultation, dyspnea, S3 heart sound, elevated CVP, dilutional hyponatremia, decreased hematocrit.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.wenckebachThink: "Wenck-E-bach" — think "longer, longer, longer, DROP, now you have a Wenckebach." For Type II: "some P
pages.nursingStudyGuides.whenMonitoringI: When monitoring IV magnesium sulfate (e.g., for preeclampsia), assess three things: deep tendon reflexes (should be present), respiratory rate (should be ≥12), and urine output (should be ≥30 mL/hr). Absent DTRs = magnesium toxicity.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.whenNotifyingTh: When notifying the provider about a critical lab value:
pages.nursingStudyGuides.whenYouReceiveA: When you receive a critical lab value, you must: (1) verify the result, (2) assess the patient, (3) notify the provider, (4) implement interventions, and (5) document the communication and actions taken.
pages.nursingStudyGuides.wideBizarreQrsC: Wide, bizarre QRS complexes followed by compensatory pause. Occasional PVCs are common and benign. Frequent PVCs (> 6/min), multifocal, R-on-T phenomenon, or runs of PVCs require intervention (amiodarone, lidocaine, address electrolytes).

pages.perioperativeHubPages.medicationMa: Medication management is a critical preoperative responsibility. The nurse must verify which medications to hold, continue, or bridge:
pages.perioperativeHubPages.npoNothingBy: NPO (nothing by mouth) guidelines reduce the risk of pulmonary aspiration during anesthesia. Follow the ASA fasting guidelines based on the type of intake.
pages.perioperativeHubPages.npoViolation: NPO violation is a common reason for surgical cancellation. Always verify NPO compliance before administering preoperative medications.

pages.perioperativeHubPages.surgicalSite: Surgical site infections (SSIs) are among the most common healthcare-associated infections. Perioperative nurses play a critical role in infection prevention through rigorous adherence to sterile technique and evidence-based protocols.
pages.perioperativeHubPages.theCertified: The Certified Perioperative Nurse (CNOR) credential is the gold standard for OR nursing excellence. Offered by the Competency & Credentialing Institute (CCI), the CNOR demonstrates advanced knowledge in perioperative patient care.
pages.perioperativeHubPages.thePreoperat: The preoperative phase is the most heavily tested domain on the CNOR exam. Focus on patient assessment workflows, surgical site marking protocols, informed consent requirements, and NPO guidelines. Remember: the circulating nurse is responsible for verifying all elements of the pre-procedure verification process.
pages.perioperativeHubPages.theWhoSurgic: The WHO Surgical Safety Checklist has reduced surgical mortality by 47% and complications by 36% in studies worldwide. Mastering each phase is essential for perioperative nursing practice and CNOR exam success.
pages.perioperativeHubPages.yourComprehe: Your comprehensive resource for mastering surgical nursing — from preoperative assessment to postoperative recovery. Aligned to the CNOR exam blueprint.

pages.pharmacologyHub.advancedPharmacolo: Advanced pharmacology for NP certification including prescribing, pharmacokinetics, and autonomous medication management.
pages.pharmacologyHub.examTrapsWhatTheyR: Exam Traps: What They Really Want You to Know
pages.pharmacologyHub.expectQuestionsAbo: Expect questions about patients who fail to disclose herbal supplement use. The correct answer involves asking specific, open-ended questions.
pages.pharmacologyHub.herbalSupplementIn: Herbal supplement interactions, perioperative safety, the 4 Gs bleeding mnemonic, CYP450 interactions, and patient teaching.
pages.pharmacologyHub.herbalSupplementsA: Herbal supplements are pharmacologically active and are NOT FDA-regulated. Always assess for interactions.
pages.pharmacologyHub.holdBetablockersIf: Hold beta-blockers if HR < 60 or SBP < 90. Hold digoxin if HR < 60. Hold ACE inhibitors if potassium > 5.0.
pages.pharmacologyHub.ivFurosemideWorksI: IV furosemide works in 5 minutes; PO takes 30-60 minutes. Know when to reassess.
pages.pharmacologyHub.mechanismfirstDrug: Mechanism-first drug explorer. Understand why side effects happen at the receptor level and predict clinical effects from first principles.
pages.pharmacologyHub.naloxoneForOpioids: Naloxone for opioids, protamine for heparin, vitamin K for warfarin, flumazenil for benzodiazepines, calcium gluconate for hyperkalemia.
pages.pharmacologyHub.ololBetablockerPri: -olol = beta-blocker, -pril = ACE inhibitor, -sartan = ARB, -statin = HMG-CoA reductase inhibitor, -pam/-lam = benzodiazepine.
pages.pharmacologyHub.pharmacologyForRnL: Pharmacology for RN licensure exams including safe medication administration, drug classes, and nursing considerations.
pages.pharmacologyHub.pharmacologyResour: Pharmacology resources for pharmacy technicians, paramedics, MLTs, and other allied health professionals.
pages.pharmacologyHub.pharmacologyWithin: Pharmacology within the RPN/LPN scope of practice, focused on safe medication administration and patient monitoring.
pages.pharmacologyHub.potassiumFatalArrh: Potassium (fatal arrhythmia), vancomycin (Red Man Syndrome). Heparin: never IM.
pages.pharmacologyHub.questionsOftenTest: Questions often test whether you know when a drug level or lab value has crossed from therapeutic to dangerous.
pages.pharmacologyHub.respiratoryPharmac: Respiratory pharmacology including bronchodilators, corticosteroids, mucolytics, and oxygen therapy medications.
pages.pharmacologyHub.safetyAlertsExamWr: Safety Alerts Exam Writers Love to Test
pages.pharmacologyHub.theBestAnswerIsAlw: The best answer is always the one that ensures the patient understands why a medication or precaution is important, not just what to do.
pages.pharmacologyHub.warfarinPtinrExtri: Warfarin = PT/INR (extrinsic pathway). Heparin = aPTT (intrinsic pathway). Never mix these up.
pages.pharmacologyHub.whenAskedWhatIsThe: When asked "what is the FIRST action," think safety. Assess before intervening. Notify provider before acting independently.

preNursing.mod.anatomyPhysiology: انسانی جسم کی ساخت اور فزیالوجی

preNursing.mod.cellBiologyDesc: خلیے کی ساخت، غشائی نقل و حمل، ہومیو سٹیسیس
preNursing.mod.cellularInjuryDesc: ہائپوکسی، اپوپٹوسس بمقابلہ نیكروسیس، خلیاتی موافقت، قابل واپسی بمقابلہ ناقابل واپسی چوٹ
preNursing.mod.chemistry: صحت کی سائنسوں کے لیے کیمسٹری
preNursing.mod.chemistryDesc: ایٹمی ساخت، بندھن، pH، حل، الیکٹرولائٹ کیمسٹری
preNursing.mod.communication: صحت کی دیکھ بھال میں مواصلات
preNursing.mod.communicationDesc: علاج معالجے کی مواصلات، SBAR، دستاویزات، ٹیم ورک
preNursing.mod.culturalCompetencyDesc: SDOH، ثقافتی عاجزی، پوشیدہ تعصب، صحت کی خواندگی، صدمے سے آگاہ دیکھ بھال
preNursing.mod.diagnosticsDesc: تصویری طریقے، ECG کی بنیادیات، لیب کی قدریں، حساسیت بمقابلہ مخصوصیت
preNursing.mod.ethicsLegalDesc: بایوایتھکس کے اصول، باخبر رضامندی، مریض کے حقوق، عمل کا دائرہ
preNursing.mod.fluidsElectrolytesDesc: جسم کے سیال کے حصے، اسموسس، الیکٹرولائٹ کے کردار، تیزاب-بیس
preNursing.mod.healthAssessmentDesc: موضوعی اور معروضی ڈیٹا، IPPA تکنیکیں، حیاتیاتی علامات، دستاویزات
preNursing.mod.healthcareStructure: صحت کی دیکھ بھال کی ساخت
preNursing.mod.healthcareStructureDesc: نگہداشت کی سطحیں، عمل کا دائرہ، بین پیشہ ورانہ ٹیم، فراہمی کے ماڈل
preNursing.mod.humanFactorsDesc: سویس پنیر کا ماڈل، علمی تعصبات، SBAR، صحیح ثقافت، غلطی کی روک تھام
preNursing.mod.infectionControlDesc: انفیکشن کی زنجیر، ہاتھوں کی صفائی، PPE، معیاری احتیاطی تدابیر
preNursing.mod.inflammationDesc: تیز اور دائمی سوزش، میڈیٹرز، بخار، زخم بھرنے کے مراحل
preNursing.mod.medicalTerminologyDesc: لفظی جڑیں، پیشوند، لاحقے، کلینیکل زبان کی تفہیم
preNursing.mod.microbiologyDesc: بیکٹیریا، وائرس، فنگی، معمول کی فلورا، منتقلی
preNursing.mod.nutritionFoundationsDesc: میکرونیوٹرینٹس، تھراپیٹک ڈائٹس، مائع کی ضروریات، غذائیت کی اسکریننگ
preNursing.mod.oxygenationDesc: ہیماگلوبن بائنڈنگ، ڈسوسی ایشن کرو، کارڈیک آؤٹ پٹ، ABG کی بنیادیات
preNursing.mod.pathophysiologyDesc: بیماری کی منطق، معاوضہ، ابتدائی بمقابلہ دیر سے علامات
preNursing.mod.physiologyDesc: فیڈبیک لوپس، مائع کے حصے، ایسڈ-بیس کی بنیادیات
preNursing.mod.researchReadingDesc: مضمون کی ساخت، تعصب کی شناخت، p-values، شواہد کی سطحیں، PICO
preNursing.mod.researchStatisticsDesc: شواہد پر مبنی طریقہ کار، مطالعہ کا ڈیزائن، شماریاتی خواندگی
preNursing.mod.studyStrategiesDesc: فعال یادداشت، وقفے وقفے سے تکرار، تصوراتی نقشہ سازی، امتحان کی منطق
preNursing.pageSubtitle: نرسنگ اسکول سے پہلے آپ کو درکار بنیادی علم تیار کریں۔ بنیادی تصورات کو سمجھنے کے لیے انٹرایکٹو بصری اسباق تیار کیے گئے ہیں۔
preNursing.readyForDeeper: گہرے کلینیکل مواد کے لیے تیار ہیں؟
prenursing.cell-bio-active-transport: ATP توانائی کی ضرورت ہے۔ ارتکاز کے میلان کے خلاف حرکت کرتا ہے۔ مثال: Na+/K+ پمپ۔
prenursing.cell-bio-desc: انٹرایکٹو ڈایاگرامز اور تصورات کی جانچ کے ذریعے انسانی جسم کے بنیادی بلاکس کو دریافت کریں۔
prenursing.cell-bio-human-cell-content: ہر خلیے میں آرگنیلز نامی خصوصی ڈھانچے ہوتے ہیں جو زندگی برقرار رکھنے کے لیے مل کر کام کرتے ہیں۔
prenursing.cell-bio-human-cell-subtitle: اہم آرگنیلز اور ان کے افعال کی شناخت کریں
prenursing.cell-bio-nursing-concept: پیتھوفزیولوجی سیکھتے وقت آپ بیماری کے میکانزم کو سیلولر ڈسفنکشن تک ٹریس کریں گے۔
prenursing.cell-bio-passive-transport: توانائی کی ضرورت نہیں۔ ارتکاز کے میلان کے ساتھ حرکت کرتا ہے۔ مثالیں: پھیلاؤ، آسموسس۔
prenursing.cell-bio-transport-principle: خلیے کی جھلیوں میں نقل و حرکت ارتکاز کے میلان کی پیروی کرتی ہے جب تک کہ اس کے خلاف جانے کے لیے توانائی خرچ نہ کی جائے۔
prenursing.cell-bio-transport-subtitle: اجزاء کیسے خلیوں میں داخل اور خارج ہوتے ہیں
prenursing.cellStructureDesc: ہر نقطے پر کلک کریں تاکہ آرگنیل کا نام ظاہر ہو۔ کیا آپ تمام 12 کی شناخت کر سکتے ہیں؟
prenursing.cellStructureLabel: خلیے کی ساخت: شناخت کے لیے کلک کریں
prenursing.organelleExplore: ہر آرگنیل کی ساخت اور کلینیکل اہمیت کی کھوج کریں
prenursing.patho-desc: ایک معالج کی طرح سوچنا سیکھیں: بیماری کے میکانزم ٹریک کریں، معاوضے کو پہچانیں اور ابتدائی اور دیر کی علامات میں فرق کریں۔
prenursing.patho-disease-content: ہر بیماری ہومیوسٹیسس کی خرابی کی کہانی ہے۔ جسم فنکشن برقرار رکھنے کے لیے معاوضہ کرتا ہے لیکن آخرکار وہ میکانزم ناکام ہو جاتے ہیں۔
prenursing.patho-exam-trap: امتحانی سوالات اکثر جانچتے ہیں کہ آپ ابتدائی (معاوضہ شدہ) بمقابلہ دیر (غیر معاوضہ شدہ) علامات سمجھتے ہیں۔
prenursing.pharm-agonist: ریسیپٹر سے جڑتا ہے اور اسے چالو کرتا ہے۔ قدرتی لیگنڈ کی نقل کرتا ہے۔ مثال: مرفین (اوپیئڈ ایگونسٹ)
prenursing.pharm-antagonist: ریسیپٹر سے جڑتا ہے اور اسے بلاک کرتا ہے۔ قدرتی لیگنڈ کو فعال ہونے سے روکتا ہے۔ مثال: نالوکسون۔
prenursing.pharm-desc: سمجھیں کہ دوائیں ریسیپٹر سطح پر جسم کے ساتھ کیسے تعامل کرتی ہیں: دوائی کی حفاظت کی بنیاد۔
prenursing.pharm-receptor-clinical: یہ جاننا کہ دوائی ایگونسٹ ہے یا اینٹاگونسٹ علاج اور ضمنی اثرات دونوں کی پیشگوئی میں مدد کرتا ہے۔
prenursing.pharm-receptor-content: زیادہ تر دوائیں خلیوں پر ریسیپٹرز سے جڑ کر کام کرتی ہیں۔ دوائی-ریسیپٹر تعامل یہ طے کرتا ہے کہ دوائی سیلولر ردعمل کو فعال کرتی ہے یا روکتی ہے۔
prenursing.pharm-receptor-subtitle: دوائیں اپنے اثرات کیسے پیدا کرتی ہیں
prenursing.pharmacokineticsSubtitle: جسم دوائی کے ساتھ کیا کرتا ہے
prenursing.phys-desc: سمجھیں کہ جسم فیڈبیک لوپس، سیال مینجمنٹ اور ایسڈ-بیس ریگولیشن کے ذریعے توازن کیسے برقرار رکھتا ہے۔
prenursing.phys-ecf: خلیوں کے باہر۔ انٹراویسکولر (پلازما) اور انٹرسٹیشل (خلیوں کے درمیان) شامل ہے۔ Na+, Cl⁻, HCO3⁻ رکھتا ہے۔
prenursing.phys-feedback-concept: بلڈ گلوکوز ریگولیشن کلاسک مثال ہے۔ کھانا گلوکوز بڑھاتا ہے → لبلبہ انسولین چھوڑتا ہے → گلوکوز خلیوں میں داخل ہوتا ہے → سطح گرتی ہے → انسولین سراو کم ہوتا ہے۔
prenursing.phys-feedback-content: زیادہ تر جسمانی ضابطہ منفی فیڈبیک استعمال کرتا ہے۔ جسم تبدیلی کا پتہ لگاتا ہے، ردعمل فعال کرتا ہے اور توازن بحال کرنے کے لیے تبدیلی کو الٹ دیتا ہے۔
prenursing.phys-feedback-subtitle: جسم کا بنیادی ریگولیٹری طریقہ کار
prenursing.phys-fluid-memory: 60-40-20 اصول: 60% جسمانی وزن پانی ہے، 40% ICF ہے، 20% ECF ہے۔
prenursing.phys-fluid-subtitle: جسم کا پانی کہاں تقسیم ہوتا ہے
prenursing.phys-icf: خلیوں کے اندر۔ K+, Mg2+, PO4³⁻ رکھتا ہے۔ سب سے بڑا سیال کمپارٹمنٹ۔
prenursing.term-desc: پیشوند، لاحقے، اور جڑ لفظوں کے ذریعے کلینیکل زبان کو سمجھیں۔
prenursing.term-how-content: زیادہ تر طبی اصطلاحات سابقوں (شروع میں شامل الفاظ کے حصے)، جڑ الفاظ (جسم کے حصے کی شناخت کرنے والا بنیادی حصہ) اور لاحقوں (حالت یا طریقہ کار بتانے والے لفظ کے آخر) کا مجموعہ ہیں۔
prenursing.term-how-title: طبی اصطلاحات کیسے کام کرتی ہیں

pta.faq.canadian: Does this cover Canadian Physiotherapy Assistant content?
pta.faq.canadianAnswer: Yes. Our content serves both US PTA and Canadian Physiotherapy Assistant students.
pta.faq.difference: What is the difference between the NPTE-PTA and NPTE-PT?
pta.faq.differenceAnswer: The NPTE-PTA is for Physical Therapist Assistants (associate degree), while the NPTE-PT is for Physical Therapists (doctoral degree).
pta.faq.freeTrialAnswer: Yes! You get 5 free practice questions and access to a free mini mock exam.
pta.faq.mockExams: How many mock exams are available?
pta.faq.mockExamsAnswer: We offer 5 PTA mock exams with timers, scoring, and topic-level performance breakdowns.
pta.faq.nptePtaAnswer: The NPTE-PTA is the national licensing exam for Physical Therapist Assistants in the US, with 200 questions and a 4-hour time limit.
pta.faq.questionCount: How many PTA practice questions do you have?
pta.faq.questionCountAnswer: NurseNest Allied has 2,000+ NPTE-PTA practice questions across 20 clinical domains.
pta.faq.studyTime: How long should I study for the NPTE-PTA?
pta.faq.studyTimeAnswer: Most PTA students study 8-12 weeks before their exam.

Fewer open tabs. NurseNest combines {{questions}} exam-style items, {{flashcards}} flashcards across {{decks}} decks, CAT-style mocks, image-backed items where available, and rationales that spell out safe vs unsafe options—in one study environment aligned to nursing, NP, and allied exams.

learner.qbank.api.signInAgain: سوالیہ بینک لوڈ کرنے کے لیے آپ کو دوبارہ سائن ان کرنے کی ضرورت ہے۔
learner.qbank.api.subscriberListBlocked: یہ اکاؤنٹ موجودہ پلان یا سیشن کے ساتھ سبسکرائبر سوال کی فہرست نہیں کھول سکتا۔
learner.qbank.api.tempUnavailable: سوالیہ بینک عارضی طور پر دستیاب نہیں ہے (ڈیٹا بیس یا سروس کا مسئلہ)۔ ایک لمحے میں دوبارہ کوشش کریں۔
learner.qbank.discovery.accessVerifyFail: موضوع کے فلٹرز تک رسائی کی تصدیق نہیں ہو سکی۔ جلد ہی ریفریش کریں۔
learner.qbank.discovery.generic: موضوع کا مینو لوڈ نہیں ہو سکا؛ تروتازہ کرنے کی کوشش کریں۔
learner.qbank.discovery.notAvailable: اس اکاؤنٹ یا پلان کے لیے موضوع کی دریافت دستیاب نہیں ہے۔
learner.qbank.discovery.serverIssue: موضوع کی فہرست لوڈ نہیں ہو سکی (عارضی سرور یا ڈیٹا بیس کا مسئلہ)۔
learner.qbank.discovery.signIn: اپنے بینک کے لیے موضوع کے فلٹرز لوڈ کرنے کے لیے سائن ان کریں۔
learner.qbank.discovery.subscriptionRequ: موضوع کے مینو کو ایک فعال رکنیت کی ضرورت ہے۔ آپ اب بھی کہیں اور پیش نظارہ استعمال کر سکتے ہیں۔
learner.qbank.empty.bankEmptyGlobal.body: سوالیہ بنک میں ابھی تک کوئی شائع شدہ اشیاء نہیں ہیں۔ یہ مواد کا فرق ہے، آپ کی رکنیت نہیں۔ اگر آپ کو یہاں ڈیٹا کی توقع ہے تو سپورٹ سے رابطہ کریں۔
learner.qbank.empty.bankEmptyGlobal.titl: ابھی تک کوئی شائع شدہ سوالات نہیں ہیں۔
learner.qbank.empty.default.body: کوئی دوسرا موضوع آزمائیں، فلٹرز صاف کریں، یا ریفریش کریں۔ اگر آپ نے حال ہی میں سبسکرائب کیا ہے تو ایک لمحہ انتظار کریں اور دوبارہ کوشش کریں۔
learner.qbank.empty.default.title: اس نظریہ میں کوئی سوال نہیں۔
learner.qbank.empty.entitlementExcludesA: شائع شدہ سوالات موجود ہیں، لیکن کوئی بھی آپ کے اکاؤنٹ کے ملک اور درجے سے مماثل نہیں ہے۔ اپنے پروفائل کا ملک اور منصوبہ چیک کریں، یا سپورٹ سے رابطہ کریں۔
کوئی سوال آپ کے پروفائل سے مماثل نہیں ہے۔
learner.qbank.empty.indeterminate.body: فہرست ایک غیر معمولی وجہ سے خالی ہے۔ فلٹرز صاف کرنے کی کوشش کریں، پھر ریفریش کریں۔ اگر یہ برقرار رہتا ہے تو، کوشش کے وقت کے ساتھ سپورٹ سے رابطہ کریں۔
learner.qbank.empty.indeterminate.title: اس نظریہ میں کوئی سوال نہیں۔
learner.qbank.empty.pathwayNotOnPlan.bod: وہ راستہ آپ کے سبسکرپشن میں شامل نہیں ہے، اس لیے اسے فلٹر کے طور پر لاگو نہیں کیا گیا۔ پاتھ وے فلٹر کو صاف کریں یا اپ گریڈ کریں۔ اگر فہرست اب بھی خالی ہے، تو نیچے کی وجہ اب بھی لاگو ہوتی ہے۔
learner.qbank.empty.pathwayNotOnPlan.tit: راستہ آپ کے منصوبے پر نہیں ہے۔
learner.qbank.empty.pathwayPoolEmpty.bod: آپ کے سبسکرپشن میں آئٹمز ہیں، لیکن کسی کو بھی اس امتحان کے راستے کے لیے ٹیگ نہیں کیا گیا ہے۔ پاتھ وے فلٹر کے بغیر "تمام عنوانات" آزمائیں، یا کوئی دوسرا راستہ چنیں۔
learner.qbank.empty.pathwayPoolEmpty.tit: آپ کے تالاب میں اس راستے کے لیے کچھ نہیں ہے۔
learner.qbank.empty.topicNoMatch.body: موضوع کا فلٹر ڈیٹا بیس کے لیبل سے بالکل میل کھاتا ہے۔ مینو یا "تمام عنوانات" سے کوئی اور موضوع منتخب کریں۔
learner.qbank.empty.topicNoMatch.title: اس عنوان کے لیبل کے لیے کوئی سوال نہیں ہے۔
learner.qbank.empty.unknownPathway.body: "{{pathwayId}}" ایک تسلیم شدہ پاتھ وے ID نہیں ہے، لہذا بینک کو اس کا دائرہ کار نہیں تھا۔ پاتھ وے فلٹر کو ہٹائیں یا اپنے مرکز سے کوئی راستہ چنیں۔
learner.qbank.empty.unknownPathway.title: نامعلوم پاتھ وے فلٹر
learner.qbank.gradeFailed: اس آئٹم کی درجہ بندی نہیں کی جا سکی۔
learner.qbank.loading: آپ کا سوالیہ بینک لوڈ ہو رہا ہے…
learner.qbank.networkError: سوالات لوڈ کرنے میں نیٹ ورک کی خرابی۔
learner.qbank.notice.topicMenuTruncated: آپ کے بینک میں {{cap}} سب سے عام عنوانات دکھا رہا ہے (موضوعات میں {{omitted}} مزید سوالات درج نہیں ہیں)۔ ڈسکوری بالٹیز کو براؤز کرنے کے لیے ٹاپک ڈرل میں "تمام عنوانات" کا انتخاب کریں۔
learner.qbank.notice.topicRelaxed: موضوع "{{topic}}" کے لیے کوئی قطعی مماثلت نہیں ہے۔ اس کے بجائے آپ کے راستے کے لیے سوالات دکھا رہا ہے۔ مزید تنگ کرنے کے لیے موضوع کا مینو استعمال کریں۔
learner.qbank.notice.weakLowConfidence: کمزور ایریا ٹاپک میپنگ اس وقت کم اعتماد ہے۔ اشیاء کی درجہ بندی جاری رکھیں اور ہم موضوع کی صف بندی کو سخت کریں گے۔
learner.qbank.notice.weakUnavailable: کمزور ایریا موڈ کو پہلے چند درجہ بندی والے آئٹمز کی ضرورت ہوتی ہے۔ جب تک آپ کا ٹاپک لیجر مکمل نہیں ہو جاتا ہم ایک ملا جلا پول دکھا رہے ہیں۔
learner.qbank.pickTopic.body: موضوع پر مبنی کوئز شروع کرنے کے لیے نیچے دیے گئے مینو سے ایک موضوع کا انتخاب کریں (حالیہ آئٹمز پہلے)۔
learner.qbank.pickTopic.title: ایک موضوع چنیں۔
learner.qbank.preset.pathwayMixed: پاتھ وے مخلوط امتحان (بے ترتیب، امتحان سے منسلک)
learner.qbank.ui.batchProgressNote: اس بیچ کی پیشرفت آپ کے براؤزر میں محفوظ کی گئی ہے تاکہ آپ ریفریش اور جاری رکھ سکیں۔ بے ترتیب اور مخلوط موڈز ان سوالات کو خارج کر دیتے ہیں جب آپ اس سیشن کو پہلے ہی دیکھ چکے ہوں جب آپ زیادہ لوڈ کرتے ہیں۔ اسکورنگ سرور پر چلتا ہے۔ جوابات کو اکیلے صفحے میں درجہ بندی نہیں کیا جاتا ہے۔
learner.qbank.ui.examShellHint: امتحان کی طرز کا سیشن: وضاحتیں اس وقت تک پوشیدہ رہتی ہیں جب تک آپ انتخاب نہیں کرتے، جیسے بند کتاب کی پیسنگ (صرف مشق)۔
learner.qbank.ui.learningHigh: اس آئٹم کے میٹا ڈیٹا سے مضبوط مماثلت۔ اگلے اقدامات اسی موضوع کے ٹریک پر رہنے چاہئیں۔
learner.qbank.ui.learningLow: صرف تخمینی روٹنگ۔ سوالیہ بینک کھولیں اور اگر نتائج ہدف سے ہٹ کر نظر آتے ہیں تو فلٹرز ٹیون کریں۔
learner.qbank.ui.learningMedium: سوال کے موضوع سے روٹ کیا گیا؛ اگر بینک چوڑا، فلٹرز کے ساتھ تنگ یا آپ کی ترجیحی نظرثانی کی قطار محسوس کرتا ہے۔
learner.qbank.ui.sessionLineIdle: اس سیشن کو ٹریک کرنے کے لیے جواب دیں اور چیک کریں۔
learner.qbank.ui.topicCodeLine: موضوع کا کوڈ: {{code}} · روٹنگ کا اعتماد {{confidence}}
learner.questions.howTo.footer: پریمیم آئٹمز اور دلیلیں سبسکرائبر کے انفرادی استعمال کے لیے ہیں۔ کاپی کرنا اسکرین پر محدود ہے۔ متن کے لیے میرے نوٹ استعمال کریں جو آپ پرنٹ یا ایکسپورٹ کر سکتے ہیں۔
learner.questions.howTo.li1: جوابات کو منتخب کریں، پھر فوری تاثرات کے لیے جواب چیک کریں کا استعمال کریں۔
learner.questions.howTo.li2: جب آپ وقت پر، مکمل سیٹ ریہرسل چاہتے ہیں تو پریکٹس امتحانات کے ساتھ جوڑیں۔
learner.questions.howTo.li3a: مزید امتحان جیسے بینک سیشن کے لیے (جب تک آپ آپٹ ان نہیں کرتے تب تک وضاحتیں پوشیدہ ہیں)، شامل کریں۔
learner.questions.howTo.li3b: اس صفحہ کے URL پر، یا استعمال کریں۔
learner.questions.howTo.title: اس بینک کو کیسے استعمال کیا جائے۔
learner.questions.subtitle.locked: ایک فعال پلان کے ساتھ شامل ہر آئٹم، موضوع کے فلٹرز، اور سیشن کے موافق جائزے کے بعد عقلیت کے ساتھ مشق کریں۔
learner.questions.subtitle.subscriber: MCQ اور SATA آئٹمز کا جواب دیں، اپنے کام کو آفیشل کلید کے خلاف چیک کریں، اور دلیلیں پڑھیں۔ کمزور علاقوں کو ڈرل کرنے کے لیے ٹاپک فلٹر کا استعمال کریں۔ اس سیشن کی پیشرفت آپ کے براؤزر میں محفوظ ہے۔
learner.questions.title: سوالیہ بینک
learner.studyPlan.subtitle.locked: آپ کے امتحان کی تاریخ، کیڈنس، اور پریکٹس سگنلز ہفتہ وار اہداف کو آگے بڑھاتے ہیں اور جب آپ کے پاس ایک فعال منصوبہ ہوتا ہے تو اگلی سفارشات کا مطالعہ کریں۔
learner.studyPlan.subtitle.subscriber: آپ کے امتحان کی تاریخ، کیڈنس اور مشق کے اشارے ہفتہ وار اہداف اور چوکیوں کو چلاتے ہیں۔ اختیاری AI ہفتہ وار ڈھانچہ اب بھی نیچے رہتا ہے۔ کنفیگر ہونے پر `AI_STUDY_PLAN_ENABLED=true` کے ساتھ فعال کریں۔ طبی مشورہ نہیں۔
learner.studyPlan.title: مطالعہ کا منصوبہ
lessonContinue.title: Continue learning

pages.examLessons.appLessonsLead: Looking for the app lesson list (subscriber)?
pages.examLessons.intro: Every hub below is scoped to one country, license track, and exam. No mixed terminology, no cross-leak between NP specialties when you set your learner pathway. Start with a preview, then unlock the full lesson with a matching plan.
pages.examLessons.metaDescription: Browse nursing lessons by country, role, and exam track (REx-PN, NCLEX-RN, NCLEX-PN, NP specialties, and more). Previews are free; full depth unlocks with the matching subscription.
pages.publicLessons.ctaCreateAccount: Create a free account to save progress
pages.publicLessons.h1: Clinical lessons for NCLEX-RN, NCLEX-PN, REx-PN, and NP
pages.publicLessons.intro: NurseNest organizes lessons by country, license level, and exam so US and Canada candidates do not mix scope language. Open the exam you are sitting, preview topics, then subscribe for full depth when you are ready.
pages.publicLessons.metaDescription: Public hub for nursing exam lessons: NCLEX-RN, NCLEX-PN (US LVN/LPN), REx-PN (Canada PN), and NP tracks. Choose a pathway hub, preview content, then continue in the app.
pages.publicLessons.metaTitle: Nursing exam lessons | NCLEX-RN, NCLEX-PN, REx-PN, NP | NurseNest
pages.publicPracticeExams.catP1: معاون راستوں کے لیے، NurseNest یہ بھی فراہم کرتا ہے
pages.publicPracticeExams.catP2: ایپ میں مشق کے ٹیسٹ، بشمول موافق (CAT-اسٹائل) سیشنز جہاں پروڈکٹ انہیں نافذ کرے۔ دستیابی راستے اور مواد کے پیک کے مطابق بدلتی ہے — سائن ان کے بعد
pages.publicPracticeExams.catP3: کھولیں تاکہ دیکھیں سبسکرپشن میں کیا شامل ہے۔ یہ سرکاری NCLEX یا بورڈ کے قواعد/شیڈول کا متبادل نہیں۔
pages.publicPracticeExams.h1: NCLEX-RN, NCLEX-PN, REx-PN, NP & Allied: timed practice exams and mock tests
pages.publicPracticeExams.intro: Sign in to run full-length timed mocks, review attempt history, and use CAT-style practice sessions where your pathway includes them. This page explains what NurseNest includes—practice, study, and test readiness—not the official NCLEX, REx-PN, or board exam.
pages.publicPracticeExams.metaDescriptio: Practice exams and computer-adaptive style sessions for nursing students. Create an account to launch mocks in the app; content is organized by RN, PN, NP, and Allied pathways.
pages.publicPracticeExams.metaTitle: NCLEX & REx-PN practice exams and mock tests | NurseNest
pages.publicPracticeExams.pathwayAlliedB: Allied: Timed practice follows allied certification framing for your hub—not nursing NCLEX content.
pages.publicPracticeExams.pathwayNpBulle: NP: Advanced practice mocks are specialty-scoped (for example FNP vs PMHNP)—always enter from your NP pathway in the app.
pages.publicPracticeExams.pathwayRnPnBul: RN / PN: Mocks and drills align to NCLEX-RN, NCLEX-PN, or REx-PN context for your region.
pages.publicPracticeExams.sectionPathway: Start from your public lessons hub if you are still choosing a track:
pages.publicPracticeExams.sectionTimedMo: Full-length or section-timed attempts with review of rationales and history—available to subscribers in the app for their entitled pathway. Use this when you want exam-day pacing and a score-oriented pass.
pages.publicQuestionBank.cardAlliedCa.in: Canadian framing for prioritization, scope, and exam-style practice for your pathway.
pages.publicQuestionBank.cardAlliedUs.in: Reasoning-heavy items and protocol edges matched to US certification contexts for your discipline.
pages.publicQuestionBank.cardAlliedUs.wh: Allied health professionals using NurseNest certification prep.
pages.publicQuestionBank.cardNpCa.includ: Pathway hub explains scope and readiness; question pools align to the Canadian NP track as published.
pages.publicQuestionBank.cardNpCa.title: Canadian NP (CNPLE track)
pages.publicQuestionBank.cardNpCa.who: Canadian NP candidates following national licensure preparation.
pages.publicQuestionBank.cardNpUs.includ: Specialty-scoped advanced practice items. Pick your board track in the pathway hub—content is not interchangeable between specialties.
pages.publicQuestionBank.cardNpUs.title: US Nurse Practitioner boards
pages.publicQuestionBank.cardNpUs.who: Advanced practice candidates preparing for board-specific NP exams.
pages.publicQuestionBank.cardPnCa.includ: REx-PN–scoped practice with Canadian context—not recycled US-only copy.
pages.publicQuestionBank.cardPnCa.title: REx-PN (Canada)
pages.publicQuestionBank.cardPnUs.includ: PN-level scope, prioritization, and medication safety with rationales tied to LVN/LPN practice.
pages.publicQuestionBank.cardPnUs.title: NCLEX-PN (US LVN/LPN)
pages.publicQuestionBank.cardPnUs.who: Practical/vocational nursing candidates in the United States.
pages.publicQuestionBank.cardRnCa.includ: Country-appropriate framing, safety and scope aligned to Canadian practice, and pathway-scoped banks.
pages.publicQuestionBank.cardRnCa.title: NCLEX-RN (Canada)
pages.publicQuestionBank.cardRnCa.who: Canadian RN candidates sitting NCLEX-RN for registration.
pages.publicQuestionBank.cardRnUs.includ: Client-needs–style items, clinical judgment practice, rationales, and topic drills scoped to US RN eligibility.
pages.publicQuestionBank.cardRnUs.title: NCLEX-RN (United States)
pages.publicQuestionBank.cardRnUs.who: US RN candidates preparing for the National Council exam.
pages.publicQuestionBank.footerTimedSets: Looking for timed sets and score review? See
for how mocks and review work in NurseNest.
pages.publicQuestionBank.h1: NCLEX-RN, NCLEX-PN, and REx-PN practice questions with rationales
pages.publicQuestionBank.intro: Work board-style items in a pathway-scoped bank: RN, PN/LVN, and NP tracks for the United States and Canada. Create a free account to practice in the app; grading and rationales stay tied to your selected exam.
pages.publicQuestionBank.metaDescription: Public overview of the NurseNest nursing question bank: practice items for NCLEX-RN, NCLEX-PN, REx-PN, and NP tracks. Sign up to practice in the app.
pages.publicFlashcardsHub.featuredEmpty: Check back soon for deck previews.
pages.publicFlashcardsHub.featuredSubtit: Sample fronts only. Full backs and every card live in the app.
pages.publicFlashcardsHub.intro: NurseNest flashcards tie to your pathway, lessons, and practice stats. Browse topics and deck previews here, then sign in for full decks, spaced repetition, and weak-area study.
pages.publicFlashcardsHub.metaDescriptio: Topic-organized nursing flashcards for NCLEX-RN, NCLEX-PN, and clinical review. Sample cards here; full study inside NurseNest.
pages.publicFlashcardsHub.topicsEmpty: Topics will appear as decks are published.
pages.publicFlashcardSlug.deckCardLine: {{count}} cards · full deck in the learner app
pages.publicFlashcardSlug.metaDeckDescri: Study {{title}} with NurseNest: {{count}} nursing flashcards. Preview sample cards; subscribe for full decks and spaced repetition.
pages.publicFlashcardSlug.metaTopicDescr: Topic “{{name}}”: curated nursing flashcard decks on NurseNest. Preview sample cards; subscribe for full decks, spaced repetition, and weak-area review tied to your exam track.
pages.publicFlashcardSlug.sampleCardsSub: Short answer previews here. Premium study includes full explanations and unlimited reviews.
pages.publicFlashcardSlug.topicIntroP1: Decks below share this topic tag. Use them to reinforce quick recall alongside
pages.publicFlashcardSlug.topicIntroP3: practice. Samples are shortened; subscribers get full cards, scheduling, and weak-area queues.
pages.forInstitutions.audience3: Program directors and faculty evaluating digital exam-prep for cohorts
pages.forInstitutions.description: Volume licensing and cohort access for nursing schools, programs, and healthcare education teams using NurseNest Core.
pages.forInstitutions.h1: Institutional pricing for programs and schools
pages.forInstitutions.intro: Give your cohort the same NCLEX-RN, REx-PN, NP, and allied exam prep as individual subscribers—with seat-based access, rollout options, and terms sized to your program.
pages.forInstitutions.nextBody: Share your program type, estimated learner count, and timeline—we will follow up with pricing and rollout options. For single learners, individual plans stay on the main pricing page.
pages.forInstitutions.offer1: Seat-based licensing aligned to cohort size and term length

tools.transfusionSafety.intro: Tap the best immediate action. Rationales summarize what to watch for on exams and on the unit.
tools.transfusionSafety.metaDescription: Practice identifying transfusion reactions and first-line nursing responses.
tools.transfusionSafety.opt2a: Rising free hemoglobin or plasma hemoglobin with decreasing haptoglobin (with clinical correlation).
tools.transfusionSafety.opt2b: Isolated platelet count drop without other signs.
tools.transfusionSafety.optA: Stop the transfusion, keep IV access with normal saline, and notify the provider and blood bank per protocol.
tools.transfusionSafety.optB: Slow the infusion to 50 mL/hr and continue monitoring.
tools.transfusionSafety.optC: Give antipyretics and continue the transfusion if vital signs stabilize.
tools.transfusionSafety.q1: A patient develops fever, chills, and flank pain 45 minutes into a red blood cell transfusion. What is your priority?
tools.transfusionSafety.q2: You suspect acute hemolytic transfusion reaction. Which lab pair best supports hemolysis in this context?
tools.transfusionSafety.rationale1: Febrile or hemolytic suspicion requires stopping the blood product, maintaining patent IV access with saline, monitoring, and escalation—before restarting anything.
tools.transfusionSafety.rationale2: Hemolysis releases free hemoglobin and consumes haptoglobin; always interpret with the clinical picture and institutional transfusion reaction pathway.
tools.transfusionSafety.title: Transfusion safety
